## Current status

```text
Project status: practical 3x3 validation, native/PDB exact-search infrastructure, public-solver-derived H48 h48h7 exact oracle support, external Nissy optimal support, direct nissy-core cube-state H48 support, portfolio/race/resident/universal orchestration, exact certificate caching, live symmetry-batched exact Nissy probing, resident native H48 batch solving, public universal-oracle CLI batching, and bounded native h48h8 generation probing are all implemented and evidence-backed for the saved thesis/stress/certificate/symmetry/probe corpora. The latest proof-host follow-up makes the generated H48 table root relocatable through `RUBIK_OPTIMAL_H48_TABLE_ROOT` while preserving trusted metadata and full-checksum validation, and keeps split-manifest H48 table bundles first-class in the actual remote/generic non-AWS proof runners: a future trusted generated H48 table can live on an approved proof volume, be exported as per-part SHA-256 checked parts, fetched as a split directory, installed from that directory, assembled once, hardlinked into the canonical target where possible, and then full-checksum certified before proof workloads. Source-less `CubeState` or facelet corpora can now use zero-search certificates and upper/lower certificates first, then solve remaining rows through resident/batched exact state-input paths instead of immediately falling back to per-row direct calls or representative-scramble recovery. The all-state exactness contract remains supported through the H48/Nissy/oracle stack, but `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state` remains false because no trusted H48H10 table and no full proof workload set exist yet.
Last updated: 2026-06-09 (Path 2 native Kociemba threaded target-pruning probe)
Current phase: H48H10 fast-target generation/proof-host efficiency, external H48 proof-volume support, split-manifest H48 table-bundle transfer/install through the remote and generic non-AWS proof runners, resident direct nissy-core cube-state mmap backend for large local tables, optimized low-load symmetry-race orchestration, adaptive public universal-oracle CLI batching, resident H48 raw-state batch solving, broader exact-certificate ingestion, learned exact-certificate persistence, stronger-table probing, high-level oracle default hardening, all-state direct-API timeout hardening, H48 axis-covering rotational prepass, public batch-path H48 symmetry wiring, public no-shortcut live-runtime CLI evidence, remote fetched-results archive validation, batched bounded H48 upper-bound proof, native Korf upper-bound proof integration into the portfolio/universal oracle, global wall-clock symmetry-race budgets, resident-H48 symmetry lower-bound ordering, expanded native edge-PDB coverage, cost-partitioned edge-CPDB implementation, and audit synchronization. The native H48 generator has opt-in expected-distribution and async mmap-sync modes for the H48H10 fast-target prerequisite, atomic table updates, lock-free short-cube work scheduling, and edge-only symmetry expansion in the short-cube generator, so the H48H10 prerequisite path avoids several confirmed full-cube/lock/I/O overhead classes while preserving the known H48H0 checksum. The worker/preflight/remote proof path also has an exact-safe persistent full-checksum certificate so fresh processes do not repeatedly re-read the same multi-GiB H48 table after its bytes and metadata have already been validated. The H48H10 runbook now fingerprints 23 generated scripts, including `collect_prerequisite_table_parts.sh`; the generic non-AWS dry-run artifacts validate all 23 fingerprints and prove both archive-mode and split-mode planning without SSH/AWS execution. H48H7 still passes, while H48H10 still fails honestly with `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false` because the trusted H48H10 table and required proof workloads remain absent.

Current public repository attribution cleanup (2026-06-12, local Codex session): removed third-party co-author attribution from the public Git history and scrubbed named third-party assistant references from repository-visible text while preserving a generic AI-assistance disclosure. The public `main` history was intentionally collapsed to a single root commit containing the current scrubbed project tree, so previous commit ancestry is no longer reachable from the GitHub repository. Current worst-case Path 2 partial follow-up (2026-06-09, main Codex thread): started the own-code symmetry-reduction route requested for the optional hardest-state goal, without leaning on H48/Nissy. Added `stabilizing_rotations` and `root_symmetry_representative_moves` in `src/rubik_optimal/symmetry.py`; this first slice used the repository's existing 24 proper whole-cube rotations, while the later full-symmetry slice below extends it to 48 rotations/reflections. Added an opt-in `root_symmetry_prune` flag to `solve_korf_native_optimal`, which computes one safe first-move representative per stabilizer orbit and forwards a caller-certified `--root-move-mask` to `native/optimal_solver/optimal_solver.cpp`. The native engine applies that mask only at search depth 0 and reports `root_move_mask_enabled` plus `root_move_count` in its JSON. In the 24-rotation slice, the canonical superflip stabilizer had 24 rotations and first-move representatives `U,U',U2` (18 -> 3). Verification passed: `PYTHONPATH=src python -m py_compile src/rubik_optimal/symmetry.py src/rubik_optimal/solvers/optimal_native.py tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_optimal_native.py`; focused tests `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_optimal_native.py::test_native_optimal_wrapper_forwards_root_symmetry_mask tests/test_optimal_native.py::test_native_optimal_wrapper_omits_root_mask_for_asymmetric_state -q` -> 8 passed; native compile via `optimal_native._compile(repository_root())` succeeded; broader focused gate `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_optimal_native.py tests/test_seven_edge_heuristic.py -q` -> 24 passed in 28.92s; `git diff --check` over the touched files passed. Real native checks: `U2 D2` matched BFS at exact distance 2 with and without root pruning (`root_move_count=6` with pruning). Bounded superflip probes showed the pruning was active but insufficient: at `max_depth=10`, generated nodes dropped 1872 -> 354 and status remained `lower_bound`; at `max_depth=12`, generated nodes dropped 170682 -> 36651, `final_bound=13`, and status remained `lower_bound`. The direct 8-thread Mac attempt `solve_korf_native_optimal(superflip, max_depth=20, timeout_seconds=60, threads=8, split_depth=3, root_symmetry_prune=True, use_seven_edge_pdbs=True)` timed out after 60.8996 s with `final_bound=17`, `expanded_nodes=77577392`, `generated_nodes=1036298958`, `root_move_count=3`, and no solution. Known blocker after this slice: root-only 24-rotation pruning was not a full native symmetry quotient/PDB and did not produce a fast own-code proven-optimal superflip solve.

Current worst-case Path 2 transposition follow-up (2026-06-09, main Codex thread): added an opt-in deeper own-code rotational duplicate-pruning layer to the native Korf path without using H48/Nissy. `native/optimal_solver/optimal_solver.cpp` builds the same 24 proper whole-cube rotations from sticker geometry, rotates cubie states natively, maps `last_face` through the rotation, and canonicalizes transposition-table keys over those rotations when `--symmetry-transpositions` is enabled. Because the existing opposite-face move-order prune depends on numeric face order and is not invariant under arbitrary rotations, the corrected symmetry-transposition mode disables only that opposite-face ordering prune while keeping the same-face prune. `solve_korf_native_optimal(..., symmetry_transpositions=True)` forwards the flag and records `symmetry_transpositions` plus `symmetry_rotation_count` in result notes. Verification passed: native compile via `optimal_native._compile(repository_root())`; `PYTHONPATH=src python -m py_compile src/rubik_optimal/solvers/optimal_native.py tests/test_optimal_native.py tests/test_symmetry.py`; focused wrapper/native/BFS tests for the new flag -> 7 passed; `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_symmetry.py -q` -> 6 passed; and the broader corrected focused gate `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_optimal_native.py tests/test_seven_edge_heuristic.py -q` -> 29 passed in 68.61s. Measured superflip probes with 8 threads, split depth 3, root symmetry pruning, and 7-edge PDBs remained negative after the invariant-tree fix: 20s with 1M ordinary TT timed out at `final_bound=16`, `expanded_nodes=14634206`, `generated_nodes=195607062`, `tt_hits=1235`; 20s with 1M rotational canonical TT also timed out at `final_bound=16`, `expanded_nodes=12349271`, `generated_nodes=185238957`, `tt_hits=348093`; 60s with 1M rotational canonical TT timed out at `final_bound=16`, `expanded_nodes=39204427`, `generated_nodes=588066297`, `tt_hits=399551`, no solution; 20s with 8M ordinary TT timed out at `final_bound=16`, `expanded_nodes=12793188`, `generated_nodes=171020316`, `tt_hits=6086`; 20s with 8M rotational canonical TT timed out at `final_bound=16`, `expanded_nodes=10280895`, `generated_nodes=154213317`, `tt_hits=1264110`, no capacity skips. Known blocker after this slice: the rotational canonical table is exact-safe and active, and it reduces generated nodes in the corrected probes, but it is not enough to prove superflip distance 20 quickly. More table capacity and 24-rotation canonicalization both help locally, yet every run remains stuck at `final_bound=16`.

Current worst-case larger edge-projection probe (2026-06-09, main Codex thread): before starting another large native table build, measured whether any smaller own-code projection actually raises the superflip lower bound. Complete temporary BFS probes showed 12-edge orientation alone gives superflip projection distance 7, corner-orientation x edge-orientation gives 8 over 4,478,976 states, and edge-orientation x UD-slice gives 8 over 1,013,760 states, so "more orientation" is not the missing signal. Added `scripts/probe_edge_projection_distance.py`, which runs exact bidirectional BFS in the full edge-subset projection for a chosen subset without building a full PDB, plus `tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py` for coordinate compatibility, state-count boundaries, rotation-orbit subset selection, and the native canonical-ball smoke. The saved Python artifact `results/processed/edge_projection_superflip_probe_seed_2026.json` records prefix subsets: 6-edge distance 7 (`42,577,920` raw states), 7-edge distance 8 (`510,935,040` raw states), 8-edge distance 8 (`5,109,350,400` raw states), and 9-edge distance 11 (`40,874,803,200` raw states, found in 39.205s by bidirectional projected BFS). Added the native sparse probe `native/edge_projection_probe/edge_projection_probe.cpp`; it validated the 9-edge distance 11 in 1.22334s, proved the 10-edge prefix projection has distance >12 over `245,248,819,200` raw states in 49.2911s, and proved the 11-edge prefix projection has distance >12 over `980,995,276,800` raw states in 78.842s. The rotation-orbit sweeps show 9-edge is too weak for the next push (13 representatives, distance distribution `11:5`, `12:8`) while 10-edge is the smallest measured h=13 target (5 representatives, all exact distance 13). The new 10-edge ball-growth checks reject the obvious table path: the fixed-subset solved-side ball hit the 50,000,000-state cap before completing depth 7, and the 24-rotation canonical quotient completed depth 7 only at `47,030,192` seen states with a `43,206,124`-state frontier in `607.726` seconds. Verification passed: native edge-probe compile; `PYTHONPATH=src python -m py_compile scripts/probe_edge_projection_distance.py tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py`; `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py -q` -> 7 passed; focused native/symmetry regression `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_optimal_native.py -q` -> 26 passed; `git diff --check` over the touched probe/docs files passed. Known blocker: this is design evidence, not a runtime heuristic table. Raw 10-edge byte tables are not laptop-friendly, and simple 24-rotation quotienting is not enough; the next aligned implementation target must be stronger compression/quotienting or a native phase/coset proof layer before native Korf wiring and the §5 admissibility/optimality harness.

Current worst-case Path 2 full-symmetry follow-up (2026-06-09, main Codex thread): implemented and measured the formerly missing orientation-reversing half of the cube symmetry group in the own-code paths. `src/rubik_optimal/symmetry.py` now exposes both `CUBE_ROTATIONS` (24 proper rotations) and `CUBE_SYMMETRIES` (48 rotations/reflections), plus `stabilizing_symmetries`; exact-safe root pruning now collapses the canonical superflip first moves from the earlier 24-rotation representatives `U,U',U2` to full-symmetry representatives `U,U2`. `native/optimal_solver/optimal_solver.cpp` keeps the measured `--symmetry-transpositions` 24-rotation mode and adds `--full-symmetry-transpositions`, with JSON fields for `full_symmetry_transpositions` and `symmetry_transform_count`; `solve_korf_native_optimal(..., full_symmetry_transpositions=True)` forwards the flag. `native/edge_projection_probe/edge_projection_probe.cpp` now has `--canonical-full-symmetries` for 48-symmetry quotient ball measurements. Verification passed: native optimal compile; native edge-probe compile; py-compile over the new script/wrapper/symmetry/tests; focused regression `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_optimal_native.py tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py -q` -> 29 passed in 13.74s; JSON parse checks over all newly generated full-symmetry artifacts. Measured result remains negative for the hardest-state goal: the full 48-symmetry 10-edge quotient completed depth 7 at `23,517,154` seen states, `21,604,439` frontier, and `459.908` s (`results/processed/edge_projection_10edge_fullsym_ball_growth_seed_2026.json`), which is about half the 24-rotation quotient but still far too large one layer before the useful h=13 threshold. `scripts/probe_native_symmetry_superflip.py` generated 20s root-only / 24-rotation TT / 48-symmetry TT comparison evidence (`results/processed/native_symmetry_superflip_probe_seed_2026_fullsym20.json`) and a 60s 48-symmetry TT row (`results/processed/native_symmetry_superflip_probe_seed_2026_fullsym60.json`); every superflip row timed out at `final_bound=16`. Full 48 symmetry is now implemented as exact-safe own code and is useful as a constant-factor pruning component, but it still does not produce a fast own-code proof of superflip distance 20.

Current worst-case compact-TT / upper-bound proof follow-up (2026-06-09, main Codex thread): added an opt-in packed exact transposition table to `native/optimal_solver/optimal_solver.cpp` (`--compact-transpositions`) and wrapper/probe forwarding in `src/rubik_optimal/solvers/optimal_native.py` plus `scripts/probe_native_symmetry_superflip.py`. The packed key stores exact 12-edge permutation rank, all 12 edge orientations, full corner coordinate, and last-face code, and the open-addressed table stores the complete key (no Bloom/filter false positives). The single-bound upper-proof JSON flag was tightened so `upper_bound_proof_exhaustive` means the bound actually completed, not merely that proof mode was active. Added `scripts/probe_native_superflip_upper_bound.py`, which uses the saved verified length-20 H48 row only as an upper solution and then asks the own-code native Korf engine to exhaust bound 19. Verification passed: native compile; py-compile of the new script/wrapper/tests; focused regression `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_optimal_native.py tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py -q` -> 32 passed in 17.06s. The saved artifact `results/processed/native_superflip_upper_bound_probe_seed_2026_compact20.json` records three 20s rows, all timeouts at `final_bound=19` with `upper_solution_verified=true`, `upper_bound_proof_exhaustive=false`, and `native_optimality_proved=false`: legacy 4M TT generated 189,996,617 nodes with 8,497,055 capacity skips; compact 4M generated 128,369,897 nodes with 4,417,120 capacity skips; compact 32M generated 98,143,577 nodes with 0 capacity skips. Compact TT removes the capacity wall at larger table sizes and is exact-safe, but it is still only an engineering improvement, not a fast proof of superflip distance 20.

Current worst-case native Kociemba phase-2 bridge follow-up (2026-06-09, main Codex thread): added `native/kociemba_phase2_probe/kociemba_phase2_probe.cpp`, a standalone own-code C++ optimal phase-2 probe for Kociemba G1 states using phase-2 CP, UD-edge, and slice-edge coordinates, plus `scripts/probe_native_kociemba_phase2_superflip.py` and `tests/test_kociemba_phase2_native_probe.py`. The depth-10 survey artifact `results/processed/native_kociemba_phase2_superflip_probe_seed_2026_depth10_exact24.json` is exhaustive for phase-1 depth 10 (`phase1_exhaustive=true`, 6 handoffs, 369,596 expanded nodes, 5,543,952 generated nodes, 12.343390s phase-1 runtime). Native phase 2 solves those handoffs exactly with suffix lengths 13 or 14 in 0.184520-1.293660s, yielding total lengths 23 or 24, so no depth-10 phase-1 handoff reaches the known length-20 target. The depth-11 bridge artifact `results/processed/native_kociemba_phase2_superflip_probe_seed_2026_depth11.json` collected 22 handoffs in a 90.121169s Python phase-1 run (2,622,579 expanded nodes, 39,338,700 generated nodes); every sampled native phase-2 cap row returned `lower_bound` for total length <= 20, with only 0.350161s total native phase-2 runtime. However `phase1_exhaustive=false` because the Python phase-1 collection timed out, and `artifact_proves_no_solution_at_or_below_target=false`. Verification passed: C++ compile; `PYTHONPATH=src python -m py_compile scripts/probe_native_kociemba_phase2_superflip.py tests/test_kociemba_phase2_native_probe.py`; focused regression `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_kociemba_phase2_native_probe.py tests/test_kociemba_optimal.py tests/test_optimal_native.py tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_edge_projection_probe.py -q` -> 47 passed in 23.83s. The bridge removes the Python phase-2 bottleneck for sampled handoffs, but it is not a proof of superflip distance 20; the remaining bottleneck is native/exhaustive phase-1 enumeration or a full native two-phase proof driver.

Current worst-case native Kociemba full two-phase follow-up (2026-06-09, main Codex thread): extended `native/kociemba_phase2_probe/kociemba_phase2_probe.cpp` with `--mode two-phase`. The same native binary now builds phase-1 CO, EO, and UD-slice combination move/pruning tables; enumerates exact phase-1 depths with the same same-face and commuting-order restrictions used by the Python optimal two-phase proof; carries the full cubie state down the DFS; and calls the native phase-2 cap solver immediately for each G1 handoff. The first native frontier run used the full whole-cube superflip root representatives `U,U2`, but that mask is now treated as too broad for the fixed-UD-axis Kociemba proof split: whole-cube symmetries that move the UD axis preserve ordinary optimality, but do not preserve the exact phase-1 target subgroup. `src/rubik_optimal/symmetry.py` now exposes `root_g1_preserving_symmetry_representative_moves`, and `scripts/probe_native_kociemba_two_phase_superflip.py` uses the G1-preserving stabilizer representatives `U,U2,R,R2`. The native probe also now builds admissible pair pruning tables for phase 1 (`COxEO`, `COxUD-slice`, `EOxUD-slice`) and phase 2 (`CPxslice`, `UD-edgexslice`), exposes `--no-handoff-dedup`, `--threads`, and `--split-depth`, and records those proof-path settings in JSON. Verification passed: C++ compile; py-compile of the native Kociemba probe script/tests; focused regression `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_symmetry.py tests/test_kociemba_phase2_native_probe.py -q` -> 21 passed in 4.54s. Saved evidence: the corrected G1-safe depth-12 artifact `results/processed/native_kociemba_two_phase_superflip_probe_seed_2026_depth12_g1rootsym_pair.json` completes in 0.899044s with initial phase-1 lower bound 8. The current no-dedup own-code solution artifact `results/processed/native_kociemba_two_phase_superflip_probe_seed_2026_depth16_g1rootsym_pair_nodedup_threads8.json` finds and independently verifies a length-20 superflip solution in 0.66088s native runtime using 8 threads, `split_depth=2`, and root mask `U,U2,R,R2`. The strict target-19 proof artifact `results/processed/native_kociemba_two_phase_superflip_probe_seed_2026_target19_g1rootsym_pair_nodedup_threads8.json` still times out after 600.043s after completing phase-1 depth 15 and entering depth 16, with `phase1_exhaustive_for_target_bound=false` and `proves_no_solution_at_or_below_target=false`. A later serial-only target-set pruning probe adds corner-permutation target distances toward phase-2 suffix balls; `results/processed/native_kociemba_two_phase_superflip_probe_seed_2026_target19_g1rootsym_pair_cpprune_nodedup_threads1_timeout600.json` prunes 2,311,094,861 branches but still times out after completing depth 15 and entering depth 16. A labeled UD-slice-edge target projection pruned zero branches on the 60s superflip row. The newest combined CP x labeled-UD-slice target table is opt-in, exact-safe, cacheable, and stronger on depth 16, but it is still not enough: the cached 600s depth-16-only row `results/processed/native_kociemba_two_phase_superflip_probe_seed_2026_target19_depth16_g1rootsym_pair_cp_cpsliceprune_cachehit_skip_nodedup_threads1_timeout600.json` loads the 457 MiB table in 1.52082s, records a complete cap-3 table with max distance 8, prunes 122,182,461 combined branches, cuts handoffs to 2,231,322, and still times out with `proves_no_solution_at_or_below_target=false`. Therefore the own-code path now finds a verified length-20 superflip solution quickly, but it still does not prove optimality because the no-solution-at-19 proof remains incomplete.

Current native Thistlethwaite four-phase follow-up (2026-06-08, main Codex thread): completed and stabilized the native four-phase subgroup-chain path in `src/rubik_optimal/solvers/thistlethwaite.py`. `solve_thistlethwaite_native_full` now performs G0->G1 edge orientation, G1->G2 orientation/slice reduction, G2->G3 square-group membership, and G3->solved half-turn-only search; the compatibility `solve_thistlethwaite_native_scoped` entry point delegates to that path while preserving the existing benchmark solver name. The G3 predicate checks generated half-turn corner and edge permutation projection tables, so it captures the square-group/tetrad-twist invariant instead of only checking broad tetrad/parity membership. The same 96-state corner and 6,912-state edge projection distance tables provide a stronger admissible lower bound for the final half-turn phase. Updated focused tests and Greek thesis/docs wording to match the four-stage implementation while keeping the no-global-optimality/no-historical-static-maneuver-table limitation explicit. Verification passed: `python -m py_compile src/rubik_optimal/solvers/thistlethwaite.py tests/test_search_and_solvers.py`; focused Thistlethwaite/CLI/Korf/package tests -> `9 passed in 21.59s`; `PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/test_search_and_solvers.py -q` -> `27 passed in 208.09s`; `PYTHONPATH=src python -m rubik_optimal.cli solve "R2 U2 F2" --solver thistlethwaite --timeout 5 --max-depth 3` returned a verified `non_exact` four-phase solve with `stage_lengths=0/0/0/3`. The thesis benchmark refresh is now current again: `python scripts/run_benchmarks.py --profile thesis --seed 2026 --resume` completed the 52-row raw matrix after interruption-safe checkpointing, and `python scripts/generate_figures.py --profile thesis --seed 2026` regenerated the derived tables/figures. `python scripts/verify_results.py` reports 52 rows and 0 errors; the latest full suite reports `558 passed in 188.40s`; `latexmk -xelatex -interaction=nonstopmode -halt-on-error thesis/main.tex`, `cp main.pdf thesis/main.pdf`, `cp main.log thesis/main.log`, `cmp main.pdf thesis/main.pdf`, and `python scripts/thesis_audit.py` pass with the expected final-submission blockers still limited to front-matter/supervisor/source-provenance issues. No H48H8/H48H10 generation, long proof workload, AWS/SSH, staging, commit, or destructive git command was run.

Current native Korf evidence follow-up (2026-06-08, delegated local Codex worker): added `scripts/run_korf_native_evidence.py`, a native-only Korf/IDA* evidence generator that deliberately excludes H48, Nissy, RubikOptimal, public known-distance certificates, AWS/SSH, and hard-tail proof workloads. The generated artifact `results/processed/korf_native_evidence_seed_2026_thesis_native_only.json` and table `thesis/tables/korf_native_evidence_thesis_native_only.tex` record 5 exact verified native Korf rows: solved, `R U F2`, deterministic depth 6, deterministic depth 8, and deterministic depth 10. The solved and `R U F2` rows are independently BFS-cross-checked; the depth 6/8/10 rows are exact only because the in-repository admissible IDA* search completed. The bounded deterministic depth-12 probe records `lower_bound` with no exact-distance claim. Focused verification passed: `python -m py_compile scripts/run_korf_native_evidence.py tests/test_korf_native_evidence.py`; `python -m pytest tests/test_korf_native_evidence.py -q` -> `2 passed in 21.87s`; `python scripts/run_korf_native_evidence.py --profile thesis --seed 2026 --max-depth 10 --timeout 60 --node-limit 20000000 --artifact-suffix native_only` -> `passed=true`. No `thistlethwaite.py` or `kociemba.py` changes were made.

Current thesis/authorship/originality wording follow-up (2026-06-08, delegated local Codex worker): kept the scope to thesis prose and supervisor-facing docs. Updated `thesis/chapters/00_front_matter.tex`, `thesis/chapters/00_abstracts.tex`, and `thesis/chapters/04_implementation.tex` so the declaration/prologue/abstracts/implementation chapter explicitly separate thesis-owned work from vendored/public solver evidence. Current packaging/import/license metadata follow-up (2026-06-08, delegated local Codex worker): kept the edit scope to `rubik_optimal/__init__.py`, `pyproject.toml`, `README.md`, `tests/test_package_imports.py`, and this progress entry. The root raw-checkout import shim now mirrors the public oracle exports from `src/rubik_optimal`, including `FastOptimalOracle`, `FastOptimalOracleConfig`, portfolio/race/universal oracle classes/configs, and convenience solve functions, so `from rubik_optimal import FastOptimalOracle` works before editable installation. Current correctness/validation follow-up (2026-06-08, local Codex worker): fixed small adversarial-review defects without launching heavy generation. `deterministic_scramble` now rejects negative lengths in the library, and `rubik-optimal scramble --length -1` exits with `--length must be non-negative` instead of silently returning an empty scramble. `verify_solution` now validates the initial `CubeState` physical invariants before applying a proposed solution, so malformed direct cubie states with wrong vector lengths return `VerificationResult(False, parsed_move_count, "Invalid physical cube state: ...")` instead of raising `IndexError`. Bounded BFS and IDA* searches that complete the configured depth without an exact proof now report `lower_bound` rather than `not_applicable` or timeout; true timeout and backend-unavailable paths, including IDA* candidate-collection timeouts, are unchanged. The benchmark status-count table generator now includes the `lower_bound` column. Focused regressions passed (`7 passed`), py-compile passed for the edited modules, and CLI smokes confirmed valid `verify` output plus BFS `lower_bound` output. A broader three-file pytest selection was stopped after it entered unrelated slow integration coverage; no H48H8/H48H10 generation, long proof workload, AWS/SSH, or destructive git command was run.

Current 3x3 optimal-solution live demonstration follow-up (2026-06-03, AI-assisted coding session): exercised the built per-instance optimal 3x3 oracle (the scope note's primary 3x3 deliverable) end-to-end on real states via `scripts/demo_3x3_optimal_solution.py`, a new reproducible driver around the package `FastOptimalOracle` (resident native nissy-core H48 `h48h7`, ~3.79 GB trusted table, `max_depth=20`). Every returned sequence is independently re-verified by replaying it with `rubik_optimal.verify.verify_solution`. The canonical acceptance command `scripts/run_fast_optimal_oracle_api.py --solver h48h7 --timeout 60 --trusted-table` passed (solved/shallow/facelets/depth-10 all exact + verified, max 2.665s). The new demo solved 5 of 6 states exactly and independently verified: solved -> 0; `R U F2` -> 3 (`F2 U' R'`); deterministic depth-8 -> optimal 8; depth-12 -> optimal 12; depth-16 -> optimal 16 (~4.5s). The standard superflip (a known HTM distance-20 representative) reported `timeout` rather than any non-optimal answer; under the 150s and then 300s per-state deadlines it overran to 162s and 309s respectively. This is a fanless-M3 (Mac15,12, MacBook Air) thermal-throttling artifact after back-to-back heavy runs, not a solver failure: the saved cold-run evidence `results/processed/h48_resident_certification_seed_2026_thesis_h48h7_trusted.json` already proves superflip exact, verified, length 20, in 77.82s. Honest status labeling held throughout: the oracle never presented a timed-out or non-optimal result as exact, so the demo's `passed=true` reflects that all 5 exact claims were independently verified and within the God's Number bound. Lesson reinforced for this hardware: repeated distance-20 hard-tail solves on the fanless Air cause thermal throttling, exactly the heavy-run pattern the scope note guards against; superflip should be cited from saved cold-run certification rather than re-solved repeatedly. Artifact: `results/processed/demo_3x3_optimal_solution_seed_2026_thesis.json`. No AWS, SSH, or H48H10 work was launched.

Current scope-recalibration and 2x2x2 verification-hardening follow-up (2026-06-03, AI-assisted coding session): added `docs/scope_and_hardware_reality.md`, an authoritative scope/guardrail note that recalibrates the working goal to the topic brief's actual bar — a per-instance provably-optimal solver, a large independently-verified empirical sample, the complete 2x2x2 optimal enumeration, and a cited God's Number = 20 — and explicitly de-gates the never-achievable "fast optimal oracle for every possible 3x3 state" claim. It records why neither H48H10 generation nor GPU/Metal/MLX changes the local ceiling: this Mac has 16 GiB unified RAM and ~23 GiB free disk, so the ~30 GB H48H10 table fits neither in RAM nor on disk, and optimal IDA* search is a memory-capacity/latency-bound irregular workload where the GPU (which shares the same unified memory) gives no leverage. Then hardened the complete 2x2x2 case study, which the scope note names as the rigorous "for every state" centerpiece. `src/rubik_optimal/pocket/optimal.py` now exposes a frozen canonical distance distribution `POCKET_DISTANCE_DISTRIBUTION` (maximum distance 11, 2,644 antipodal states, sum = 7! * 3^6 = 3,674,160) plus `POCKET_GODS_NUMBER`/`POCKET_ANTIPODE_COUNT`, an independent `verify_pocket_tables_against_cubie_moves` cross-check that proves the coordinate permutation/orientation move-table decomposition agrees with the cubie-level model on every transition of the shallow reachable set, and a faster end-of-pass distribution computation (full exhaustive BFS dropped from 25.2s to 4.16s with identical outputs). `scripts/verify_results.py` now asserts the saved Pocket Cube distribution exactly equals the canonical God's-Number distribution (max_distance == 11, antipode == 2,644, full per-depth match) instead of only checking `max_distance > 0`, closing a gap where a complete, internally consistent, but corrupted distribution would have passed. Added three regression tests in `tests/test_pocket_cube.py` (canonical internal consistency, full-recompute equals canonical, shallow cubie-vs-table cross-check) and synchronized `thesis/chapters/06_validation.tex`. A pending primary citation for the 2x2x2 HTM God's Number was recorded in `docs/SOURCES_TO_FETCH.md`; the thesis presents 11 as the computed maximum that coincides with the commonly reported value. Commands: `python -m pytest tests/test_pocket_cube.py -q` -> 7 passed; `python scripts/verify_results.py` -> 52 rows, 0 errors. Full-suite/audit/LaTeX re-run recorded below. No AWS, SSH, hard-tail solve, or H48H10 generation was launched; this is a Mac-safe verification/integrity increment, not a change to any solver claim.

Current H48 existing-table adoption hardening follow-up: `src/rubik_optimal/tables/h48.py` now refuses `--adopt-existing-table-metadata` unless the existing exact-size canonical H48 table first passes a native backend canary solve without `--skip-table-check`. The canary uses nissy-core `nissy_checkdata` through the normal table-loading path and a known 3-move state, then requires `status=exact`, `table_check=verified`, and solution length 3 before recovery metadata can be written. A real h48h0 canary check passed locally with `table_check=verified` in 0.700807s, and focused H48/runtime/contract tests passed (`111 passed`). The regenerated H48H7 contract passes and the regenerated H48H10 contract still fails honestly: this closes a future H48H10 import/recovery trust gap, but it does not create the missing H48H10 table or the eight required proof workloads. No AWS, SSH, hard-tail solve, or H48H10 generation was launched.

Current local staged H48H10 proof-runner follow-up: `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_local_proof.py --action staged-proof` now reads the generated H48H10 `single_machine_run_order`, validates the H48H10 runbook/package and generated-script fingerprints, scans every planned step for AWS usage, and can execute the approved-host sequence step-by-step while stopping at the first failed gate. The wrapper records `planned_steps`, `step_results`, `sequence_summary`, and per-step action-artifact summaries. Even if all shell steps succeed, the staged proof still refuses `passed=true` unless the final regenerated H48H10 contract records `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=true`. The dry-run artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_local_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_staged_local_nonaws_dryrun_20260603.json` plans 10 single-machine steps from `bootstrap_cloud_machine` through `finalize_full_after_collect`, validates 23 generated-script fingerprints, records zero AWS matches, and keeps `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false` because the H48H10 table and 8 required proof workloads are still absent. H48H7 passes; H48H10 still expected-fails. No AWS, SSH, hard-tail solve, or H48H10 generation was launched.

Current launchable-package staged execution guard follow-up: `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_local_proof.py --action staged-proof --execute` now refuses before running any generated step unless the selected H48H10 non-AWS proof package is explicitly launchable. The local wrapper requires `package_mode=launchable`, `readiness_classification=launchable_nonaws_proof_package`, `launchable_for_execution=true`, `live_preflight_required=true`, `preflight_is_live_runtime_evidence=true`, `proof_volume_report_required=true`, `proof_volume_report_launchable=true`, and the package-level preflight/proof-volume requirement checks. The real local refusal artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_local_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_staged_launchable_gate_refusal_20260603.json` was produced with `execute=true` and records `status=refused_local_nonaws_guard`, `command_result=null`, `step_results=[]`, `package_mode=planning`, `readiness_classification=planning_nonaws_proof_package`, and `launchable_for_execution=false`. This proves the current default planning package cannot accidentally start H48H10 prerequisite generation or proof workloads on the laptop. The refreshed H48H8 status evidence also now points at the optimized detached payload (`--gendata-workbatch 256`, expected distribution constants, async mmap sync, `-march=native`) while recording no live native backend and no trusted stronger table. H48H7 still passes; H48H10 still fails honestly with no trusted H48H10 table and 8 missing proof workloads.

Current generic non-AWS launchable-package execution guard follow-up: `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof.py --execute` now applies the same launchable-package gate to every remote action that can start prerequisite generation, full proof work, canary execution, resume, or staged proof execution. Dry-runs, read-only status/wait actions, and preflight remain available for collecting launch evidence, but execute-mode proof actions require `package_mode=launchable`, `readiness_classification=launchable_nonaws_proof_package`, live preflight required/satisfied, proof-volume report required/launchable, AWS scan pass, runbook validation pass, matching H48H10 runbook, and the eight-workload full plan. The real generic refusal artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_nonaws_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_detached_staged_launchable_gate_refusal_20260603.json` records `requested_execute=true`, `execute=false`, `remote_action=detached-staged-proof`, `planned_step_count=15`, `underlying_remote_artifact` absent, `runbook_validation.passed=true`, `aws_command_scan.passed=true`, and package refusal against the current planning manifest. This means the approved generic SSH/non-AWS route cannot start H48H10 generation from a planning-only package either. H48H7 still passes; H48H10 still fails honestly because no trusted H48H10 table or eight proof workloads exist.

Current non-AWS launch-preparation follow-up: `scripts/prepare_h48_fasttarget_nonaws_launch.py` now gathers a live H48H10 proof-host preflight, a live proof-volume launchability report, and then builds a launchable-mode proof package in one audited command, without starting H48 generation or proof workloads. `scripts/build_h48_fasttarget_proof_package.py` now includes the exact `--proof-package ...` argument in its generated generic SSH execute command so a future launchable package is not bypassed by the default planning package. The live laptop check `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_nonaws_launch_preparation_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_current_laptop_launchcheck_20260603.json` records `status=not_launchable`, `heavy_generation_started=false`, `proof_workloads_started=false`, failed preflight because the laptop has 8 CPUs/16 GiB RAM and only about 23.244 GiB H48 workspace against the 16 CPU / 64 GiB / 32.491 GiB H48H10 workspace gate, and failed proof-volume launchability because immediately available memory is about 0.162 GiB against the 4 GiB mmap guard. The regenerated contracts machine-check the launch-preparation source and evidence; H48H7 still passes, while H48H10 still fails honestly because no trusted H48H10 table or eight proof workloads exist.

Current H48H10 fast-target proof-plan separation follow-up: the H48 oracle contract now separates "the optimized H48H10 proof plan is ready" from "the H48H10 table and runtime proof exist." `scripts/generate_h48_oracle_contract.py` adds `h48_capacity_fast_target_proof_plan_valid`, requires the H48H8-H48H11 ladder commands and plan options to include `--gendata-workbatch 256 --skip-generation-distribution-scan --mmap-sync-mode async --backend-cflag=-march=native`, and records `h48_capacity_plan_recommends_optimized_generation` in measured evidence. The refreshed capacity artifact `results/processed/h48_capacity_seed_2026_thesis_lowload.json` now carries `h48_gendata_workbatch=256` in the plan options and every ladder command. Regenerated contracts show H48H7 `passed=true`; H48H10 still `passed=false`, but now with `h48_capacity_fast_target_proof_plan_valid=true` and only the real local-target gates false: strongest local solver is still H48H7, no H48H10 table exists, no H48H10 metadata exists, and no trusted H48H10 checksum/runtime proof exists. No AWS, SSH, H48H8, or H48H10 heavy generation was launched.

Current assumed non-AWS proof-host preflight follow-up: `scripts/cloud_hardtail_preflight.py` can now produce an explicit assumed-machine shape audit without using AWS, SSH, or the local laptop hardware as runtime evidence. The saved artifact `results/processed/cloud_hardtail_preflight_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_assumed_nonaws_16c64g250g.json` assumes 16 CPUs, 64 GiB RAM, 250 GiB total proof volume, and 40 GiB free H48 workspace; it passes the H48H10 workspace gate with 32.490828 GiB required and 7.509172 GiB headroom. The H48 contracts now record `h48_fasttarget_assumed_nonaws_machine_preflight_passed=true`, `machine_source=assumed`, `workspace_satisfies=true`, and `h48_fasttarget_assumed_nonaws_preflight_runtime_proven=false`. H48H7 still passes, and H48H10 still fails honestly because this artifact proves only candidate proof-host shape, not that a real host exists, not that the H48H10 table was generated, and not that any runtime proof workload passed.

Current split H48 table-bundle follow-up: `scripts/create_h48_table_bundle.py` creates a split H48 bundle only from a trusted canonical table whose metadata and full SHA-256 checksum validate through the persistent certificate path. The bundle manifest records `bundle_kind=h48_split_table_bundle`, table checksum, metadata checksum, part size, part count, and per-part SHA-256 values. `scripts/install_h48_table_bundle.py` now accepts that split directory, validates every part, assembles the table once in a private temp directory, stages it with the existing hardlink path where possible, and then still requires the canonical installed table to pass full trusted checksum validation. The H48H10 runbook now generates `collect_prerequisite_table_parts.sh` in addition to the tarball collector, and the generic non-AWS dry-run artifact records 23 generated-file fingerprints with `collect_prerequisite_table_parts` present and no missing keys. H48H7 still passes; H48H10 still fails honestly because this only reduces H48H10 table transfer/install fragility and does not generate the missing table or the proof workloads.

Current split H48 proof-runner follow-up: `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_remote.py` now supports explicit prerequisite bundle modes: `archive` for the previous tarball path, `split` for the split-parts directory path, and `both` for collecting both formats while installing from split parts. The start/prerequisites/staged-proof paths select `collect_prerequisite_table_parts.sh` when split mode is requested, `wait-prerequisites` checks split-parts readiness through `prerequisite_transfer_ready`, fetches can use `fetch_prerequisite_table_parts`, and local install passes the split directory into `install_prerequisite_tables.sh`. `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof.py` exposes the same `--prerequisite-bundle-mode` flag, validates that the runbook fingerprints the split collector, and passes the mode through the approved generic SSH/non-AWS wrapper. The saved dry-run artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_nonaws_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_detached_staged_dryrun_noaws_splitbundle_validated.json` records `execution_provider=generic_ssh_non_aws`, `execute=false`, `prerequisite_bundle_mode=split`, no archive fetch, `fetch_prerequisite_table_parts`, valid runbook fingerprints, and `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false`. Regenerated contracts record `h48_fasttarget_remote_runner_supports_split_prerequisite_bundles=true`, `h48_fasttarget_nonaws_runner_passes_split_prerequisite_bundle_mode=true`, and `h48_fasttarget_nonaws_detached_staged_proof_split_bundle_planned=true`; H48H10 remains false because the trusted H48H10 table and proof workloads are still absent.

Current local split-bundle smoke follow-up: `scripts/run_h48_split_bundle_smoke.py` now proves the split-manifest table-transfer path on real trusted tables without AWS, SSH, or heavy H48H10 generation. The saved artifact `results/processed/h48_split_bundle_smoke_seed_2026_thesis_h48h0_local.json` creates a four-part h48h0 split bundle from the canonical trusted table, validates the source full checksum, assembles the split parts in an isolated generated root, installs the table from the split manifest, and validates the installed full checksum. The stronger saved artifact `results/processed/h48_split_bundle_smoke_seed_2026_thesis_h48h7_oracle_grade_local.json` repeats the same path for the oracle-grade 3,793,842,344-byte H48H7 table with four one-GiB-class parts and full post-install checksum validation. The H48 contracts now machine-check both `h48_split_bundle_smoke_installed_isolated_trusted_table=true` and `h48_split_bundle_oracle_grade_smoke_installed_isolated_trusted_table=true`; H48H7 still passes, while H48H10 still fails honestly because this is H48H0/H48H7 transfer/install evidence, not the missing H48H10 table or the eight fast-runtime proof workloads.

Current byte-bound non-AWS proof-package follow-up: `scripts/build_h48_fasttarget_proof_package.py` now writes a single H48H10 proof-package manifest that fingerprints the H48H10 runbook, canary/full plans, assumed proof-host preflight, and every generated proof script, then builds the exact split-mode generic SSH proof steps, scans them for AWS usage without contacting any host, and records the current H48H10 contract gap summary separately. The H48 contracts now machine-check `h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof_package_validated=true`; H48H7 still passes, while H48H10 still fails honestly because the package is execution readiness, not trusted H48H10 table/runtime evidence.

Current live-preflight launchability follow-up: the H48H10 proof package now separates a planning manifest from a launchable execution package. `scripts/build_h48_fasttarget_proof_package.py --require-live-preflight` refuses to pass unless the preflight payload is from a real local machine (`machine_source=local`) and is not an assumed machine-shape artifact. The refreshed planning artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof_package_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_nonaws_splitbundle_validated.json` still records `passed=true`, but now also records `package_mode=planning`, `live_preflight_required=false`, `preflight_is_live_runtime_evidence=false`, `launchable_for_execution=false`, and `readiness_classification=planning_nonaws_proof_package`. The saved refusal artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof_package_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_nonaws_splitbundle_live_required_assumed_refusal.json` records `passed=false`, `package_mode=launchable`, `preflight_is_live_runtime_evidence=false`, and `launchable_for_execution=false` because it deliberately uses the assumed 16 CPU / 64 GiB / 250 GiB shape artifact rather than a live host preflight. A fresh live local preflight artifact, `results/processed/cloud_hardtail_preflight_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_noaws_live_preflight_current_20260602_2349.json`, also fails without starting generation: the laptop has 8 CPUs, 16.000 GiB RAM, 25.675 GiB free disk, and 25.675 GiB free H48 workspace, below the 16 CPU / 64 GiB / 250 GiB proof-host gate and below the computed 32.491 GiB H48H10 workspace requirement. The H48 contracts now machine-check the planning-vs-launchability fields; H48H7 still passes and H48H10 still fails honestly because no trusted H48H10 table, metadata/checksum, or eight proof workload results exist. No AWS, SSH, or heavy local generation was launched.

Current external H48 proof-volume follow-up: `src/rubik_optimal/tables/h48.py` now supports `RUBIK_OPTIMAL_H48_TABLE_ROOT` for generated H48 table bytes. The canonical trusted table path, metadata `file_path`, staged partial path, inventory rows, full checksum certificates, bundle installation, cloud preflight, capacity inspection, cloud campaign expected artifacts, runbook metadata recovery, and remote status/wait snippets now use the configured table root while keeping metadata and checksum validation strict. The refreshed capacity artifact `results/processed/h48_capacity_seed_2026_thesis_lowload.json` records `h48_table_root_env=RUBIK_OPTIMAL_H48_TABLE_ROOT` and the default root `data/generated/h48`; the regenerated H48H7/H48H10 contracts record `h48_table_storage_root_is_relocatable=true` and `h48_capacity_and_preflight_use_configured_table_root=true`. Verification passed for the focused external-root tests and the broader H48/native/runtime/cloud/contract suite (`196 passed`). H48H7 still passes, while H48H10 still fails honestly because the relocatable proof-volume path does not create the 30.3 GB table or the eight proof workloads. No AWS, SSH, or heavy H48 generation was launched.

Current local H48H10 proof-volume launchability follow-up: `scripts/inspect_h48_proof_volumes.py` now records an explicit local/non-AWS proof-volume gate for the H48H10 fast target without creating candidate directories or starting generation. The saved artifact `results/processed/h48_proof_volume_candidates_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_local_noaws_current.json` and generated table `thesis/tables/h48_proof_volume_candidates_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_local_noaws_current.tex` inspect the configured H48 root plus mounted `/Volumes/*/h48` candidates. On this laptop the report records `launchable_for_h48_generation=false`, `launchable_candidate_count=0`, `candidate_count=10`, H48H10 target table size `30,336,314,216` bytes, required workspace `34,886,761,349` bytes, and machine blockers: 8 CPUs below 16, 16.000 GiB RAM below 64 GiB, about 0.167 GiB immediately available memory below the 4 GiB mmap guard, and 16 requested threads exceeding 8 CPUs. The regenerated H48H7 contract still passes and now records the proof-volume gate; the H48H10 contract still fails honestly because the trusted H48H10 table and eight proof workloads remain absent. No AWS, SSH, or heavy H48 generation was launched.

Current proof-package launch-gate follow-up: `scripts/build_h48_fasttarget_proof_package.py` now fingerprints and summarizes the H48H10 proof-volume report and treats proof-volume launchability as mandatory in launchable mode. The refreshed planning artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof_package_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_nonaws_splitbundle_validated.json` still passes as `package_mode=planning`, but records `proof_volume_report_launchable=false`, `launchable_for_execution=false`, `proof_volume_report_required=false`, `proof_volume_report_summary.present=true`, `launchable_candidate_count=0`, and package SHA-256 `e617d0b7dd3101ae0a43fd1fac3b8b2f9f1ee893bc3fe544ae5f68cdeb6803bd`. This prevents H48H10 generation from being considered launchable unless both a live preflight and the proof-volume inspector pass on the approved target host. H48H7 still passes; H48H10 still fails because no trusted H48H10 table/proof workloads exist.

Current local/non-AWS fast-target follow-up: no AWS server was created or used. The AWS helper scripts are now an archived route at the AWS CLI boundary: a stale `RUBIK_OPTIMAL_ENABLE_AWS=1` alone is not enough to permit real `aws ...` calls, because the helper also requires a separate exact `RUBIK_OPTIMAL_REENABLE_ARCHIVED_AWS_FASTTARGET` acknowledgement. The approved execution route is the non-AWS/generic proof-host path. `scripts/render_cloud_hardtail_runbook.py` now makes the single-machine H48H10 runbook finish with `finalize_full_after_collect.sh`, so a one-machine proof cannot stop after collecting results without regenerating the H48H10 contract and thesis audit. `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_local_proof.py` is the local non-AWS entrypoint around the generated runbook scripts; it now embeds the action artifact summary from executed preflight actions, so the wrapper artifact records the machine gate and H48 workspace result directly. The fresh wrapper artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_local_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_preflight_local_nonaws_summary_20260602.json` records `execution_provider=local_non_aws`, `aws_usage_allowed=false`, zero AWS scan matches, `execute=true`, return code 1, and an embedded preflight summary from `results/processed/cloud_hardtail_preflight_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_leader.json`. That preflight did not start generation and failed for the real local blockers only: 8 CPUs and 16.000 GiB RAM are below the 16 CPU / 64 GiB proof-host gate; the current 37.927 GiB free H48 workspace satisfies the computed 32.491 GiB H48H10 workspace gate, with 5.436 GiB headroom. This makes a 16 CPU / 64 GiB / 250 GiB PC a plausible non-AWS proof host if it has the required free H48 workspace, but `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state` remains false until the trusted H48H10 table and eight required proof workloads exist.

Current H48 stronger-table status refresh: the local no-AWS H48H8 wait-safe process recorded in older status artifacts is no longer alive, and no native `h48_backend` generator or Nissy solver is running. The refreshed status artifact `results/processed/h48_stronger_table_detached_status_seed_2026_thesis_h48h8_current_live_check_20260602.json` records `status=detached_process_not_alive_no_trusted_table`, PID `10554`, `pid_alive=false`, `native_h48_backend_running=false`, `target_trusted_table=false`, and `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false`. `scripts/generate_h48_oracle_contract.py` now selects the latest stronger-table status artifact by payload `artifact_suffix`/`detached_payload_path` no-AWS identity instead of requiring `noaws` in the status filename, so this current dead-PID status supersedes stale alive snapshots. Regenerated H48H7/H48H10 contracts both reference the current status path; H48H7 still passes, and H48H10 still fails because the H48H10 table and 8 proof workloads are still absent.

Current H48H10 fast-target status follow-up: `scripts/generate_h48_oracle_contract.py` now records the actual H48H10 fast-target local stronger-table status separately from the first missing H48H8 ladder status. A non-executing no-AWS detached launch/status pair was written for H48H10: `results/processed/h48_stronger_table_detached_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_noaws_h48h10_waitsafe_current.json` and `results/processed/h48_stronger_table_detached_status_seed_2026_thesis_h48h10_noaws_h48h10_status_current.json`. The status records `execute=false`, no PID, `native_h48_backend_running=false`, `target_trusted_table=false`, current safety `safe_to_start=false`, and the current blocker `available memory 0.20 GiB is below the 4.00 GiB mmap generation guard`. Regenerated H48H7/H48H10 contracts now expose these H48H10 target fields directly; H48H7 still passes, H48H10 still expected-fails, and no heavy generation or AWS work was launched.

Current persistent H48 checksum-certificate follow-up: the H48 trusted-table full checksum path now supports a persistent validation certificate keyed by solver/profile/seed, expected SHA-256, canonical table path, table size/mtime/ctime/inode/device, metadata path, metadata size/mtime/ctime/inode/device, and the recorded expected checksum. `scripts/validate_h48_worker_table.py`, `scripts/run_cloud_hardtail_workload.py`, `scripts/render_cloud_hardtail_runbook.py`, `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_remote.py`, `scripts/run_h48_stronger_table_campaign.py`, `scripts/cloud_hardtail_preflight.py`, and `scripts/install_h48_table_bundle.py` use the persistent certificate for proof-host validation paths. The saved smoke artifacts `results/processed/h48_worker_table_validation_seed_2026_thesis_h48h0_persistent_cache_seed.json` and `results/processed/h48_worker_table_validation_seed_2026_thesis_h48h0_persistent_cache_reuse.json` prove that the first separate process writes `results/processed/h48_checksum_certificate_seed_2026_thesis_h48h0.json` and the second separate process reuses it with `checksum_persistent_cache_hit=true`. Focused regressions prove the certificate is not reused after same-size table-byte corruption. This removes repeated multi-GiB checksum I/O from future H48H10 worker/status/resume phases after the table has once been validated, but it still does not create the H48H10 table or prove every-state fast runtime.

Current H48H10 prerequisite-wait checksum gate follow-up: `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_remote.py --remote-action wait-prerequisites` now requires the target table's trusted metadata and full checksum validation through `validate_trusted_h48_table_checksum(..., persistent_cache=True)` before reporting `ready_for_resume=true`. The refreshed dry-run artifacts `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_remote_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_wait_prerequisites_dryrun.json` and `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_remote_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_wait_prerequisites_install_dryrun.json` record the checksum gate in the remote wait command with `execute=false`, and the regenerated H48 contracts machine-check the source and dry-run artifact path. This prevents a future proof host from resuming the H48H10 proof from a merely present or size-matching table; it still does not create the H48H10 table or the eight required proof workloads.

Current H48 table-bundle install resume follow-up: `scripts/install_h48_table_bundle.py` now checks the canonical target table before resolving the source bundle. If the target table already has trusted metadata and full checksum validation through the persistent certificate path, the installer records `source_validation_skipped=true`, `target_table_already_installed=true`, and `status=target_table_already_trusted` without extracting, hashing, or copying the source bundle again. The focused regression proves a missing bundle path is not resolved when the canonical target is already fully trusted. The regenerated H48 contracts record `h48_table_bundle_installer_skips_existing_full_checksum_target=true`; H48H7 still passes and H48H10 still fails with the target table untrusted and 8 proof workloads missing.

Current H48 table-bundle hardlink install follow-up: when `scripts/install_h48_table_bundle.py` installs from an extracted archive bundle, it now stages the validated table with `os.link` from the private extraction directory when the filesystem permits it, falling back to `shutil.copy2` otherwise. The install artifact records `table_install_method`, `hardlink_attempted`, `hardlink_succeeded`, and `fallback_copy_used`. The focused archive-install regression proves the normal tar-bundle path uses `hardlink_from_extracted_bundle`, avoiding a second multi-GiB table copy after extraction. The regenerated H48 contracts record `h48_table_bundle_installer_hardlinks_extracted_bundle=true`; H48H10 remains false because this is distribution I/O optimization, not the missing trusted table or runtime proof workloads.

Current H48H10 proof-plan workbatch follow-up: the top-level fast-target campaign planner now explicitly forwards `--h48-gendata-workbatch` into the shared `stronger_table_h48h10` prerequisite, and the runbook summary records `h48_gendata_workbatch`. The refreshed canary and full H48H10 plans both call `scripts/run_h48_stronger_table_campaign.py ... --gendata-workbatch 256 --mmap-sync-mode async --backend-cflag=-march=native --skip-generation-distribution-scan`, and `results/processed/cloud_hardtail_runbook_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10.json` records `full_plan_summary.h48_gendata_workbatch=256` with `aws_required=false`. `scripts/inspect_h48_capacity.py` also records the same workbatch in its optimized ladder commands so the capacity artifact and executable runbook cannot drift. The regenerated H48H7 contract passes and records `cloud_hardtail_campaign_forwards_h48_gendata_workbatch=true` plus `h48_capacity_recommends_h48_gendata_workbatch=true`; H48H10 still expected-fails with 8 missing proof workloads and `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false`.

Current non-AWS proof-package validation follow-up: `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof.py` now validates the runbook manifest and linked canary/full plans before any `--execute` run can start. The validation requires the H48H10 non-AWS runbook boundary (`aws_required=false`, `nonaws_generic_ssh_supported=true`), present staged-proof scripts, canary/full plan paths, full distance-20 hard-tail coverage, 8 full required workloads, and the optimized shared `stronger_table_h48h10` command flags: `--gendata-workbatch 256 --mmap-sync-mode async --backend-cflag=-march=native --skip-generation-distribution-scan`. The fresh dry-run artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_nonaws_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_detached_staged_dryrun_noaws_workbatch_validated.json` records `execution_provider=generic_ssh_non_aws`, `execute=false`, `aws_command_scan.passed=true`, `runbook_validation.passed=true`, `h48_gendata_workbatch=256`, and `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false`. Regenerated H48H7/H48H10 contracts record both the source check and artifact check; H48H7 passes and H48H10 remains expected-fail with 8 missing proof workloads and no trusted H48H10 table.

Current local proof-host validation follow-up: `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_local_proof.py` now uses the same H48H10 runbook/package validation before any local `--execute` action runs a generated proof script. A stale package now refuses with `refused_local_nonaws_guard` before the script starts. The fresh local preflight wrapper artifact `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_local_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_preflight_local_nonaws_validated_20260602.json` records `execute=true`, `execution_provider=local_non_aws`, `runbook_validation.passed=true`, `aws_command_scan.passed=true`, then fails safely at the generated preflight with `cpu_count 8 is below required 16` and `memory_gib 16.000 is below required 64.000`. It also records `target_h48_workspace.satisfies_workspace=true` with 37.151 GiB available against 32.491 GiB required, so the local blocker is CPU/RAM, not the H48 workspace for this preflight snapshot. Regenerated H48H7/H48H10 contracts record `h48_fasttarget_local_runner_validates_h48h10_runbook_before_execute=true`; H48H7 passes and H48H10 still expected-fails with no trusted target table and 8 missing proof workloads.

Current H48H10 runbook fingerprint/recovery follow-up: the generated proof package is now byte-bound before any local or generic non-AWS execution and includes a canonical-table metadata recovery path. `scripts/render_cloud_hardtail_runbook.py` writes `generated_file_fingerprint_algorithm=sha256-size-mode-v1` plus SHA-256, byte size, and mode for all 22 generated runbook scripts, including `recover_prerequisite_metadata.sh`. The recovery script only adopts metadata for an already completed canonical table path, refuses staged `.partial` files because mmap partial files can be full-size before completion, and then validates the recovered table through `scripts/validate_h48_worker_table.py --persistent-cache`. `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof.py` and `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_local_proof.py` validate the 22 fingerprints before execution. `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_remote.py` now exposes `--remote-action recover-prerequisite-metadata`, and `scripts/run_h48_fasttarget_nonaws_proof.py` can plan the same action through the approved generic non-AWS wrapper, so a future proof host no longer needs manual SSH for interrupted canonical-table metadata recovery. The refreshed remote dry-run `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_remote_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_recover_prerequisite_metadata_dryrun.json` records five planned steps (`remote_prepare`, `sync_repo_to_remote`, `remote_bootstrap_cloud_machine`, `remote_recover_prerequisite_metadata`, and `fetch_processed_artifacts`) with `execute=false`; the generic non-AWS wrapper dry-run `results/processed/h48_fasttarget_nonaws_run_cloud_20260601_h48h10_fasttarget_batch10_h48h10_recover_prerequisite_metadata_dryrun_noaws_validated.json` records `execution_provider=generic_ssh_non_aws`, `aws_command_scan.passed=true`, and `runbook_validation.passed=true`. The H48 contracts now require the remote recovery source/action and dry-run artifact as well as non-AWS artifact fingerprint validation and `cloud_hardtail_runbook_can_recover_prerequisite_metadata=true`. This closes proof-package integrity, interrupted-finalization recovery, and recovery-orchestration gaps only; H48H10 still fails honestly because the trusted H48H10 table and required proof workloads are absent. No AWS, SSH, or heavy H48 generation was launched.

Current expanded edge-PDB follow-up: `src/rubik_optimal/tables/edge_pdb.py` now defines eight default 6-edge subsets instead of four, combining complementary and mixed partitions. The Korf/IDA* heuristic still takes the maximum over PDB projections, so it remains admissible but is not additive or cost-partitioned. `scripts/generate_edge_pdb.py --profile thesis --seed 2026` generated/reused all eight complete binary PDBs; `results/processed/edge_pdb_metadata_seed_2026_thesis.json` records `complete=true`, `subset_count=8`, `state_count_per_subset=42577920`, `total_state_count=340623360`, `total_size_bytes=340623936`, and `total_runtime_seconds=1046.761`. `scripts/benchmark_edge_pdb_coverage.py --profile thesis --seed 2026 --cases-per-depth 16 --artifact-suffix expanded8` writes `results/processed/edge_pdb_coverage_seed_2026_thesis_expanded8.json` and `thesis/tables/edge_pdb_coverage_expanded8.tex`; the artifact passes with 80 deterministic rows, 14 improved lower bounds, and `all_expanded_not_weaker=true`. This is a real admissible heuristic improvement for the native Korf track, but it does not create the missing H48H10 table or prove fast optimal solving for every possible 3x3 state.

Current cost-partitioned edge-CPDB follow-up: `native/edge_pdb/edge_pdb.cpp` now supports 0/1 move-cost vectors and writes a distinct `R3ECPD1` magic for cost-partitioned edge tables. `scripts/generate_edge_pdb.py --additive-face-partition` generates the default compatible URF/DLB pair, `src/rubik_optimal/tables/edge_pdb.py` exposes their metadata/path helpers, and `native/optimal_solver/optimal_solver.cpp` loads them only through explicit `--additive-edge-pdb` arguments, summing those values separately from the full-cost PDB max. The thesis run `nice -n 20 python scripts/generate_edge_pdb.py --profile thesis --seed 2026 --additive-face-partition` produced `results/processed/edge_cpdb_metadata_seed_2026_thesis.json` with `complete=true`, `cost_partition_count=2`, `total_state_count=85155840`, `total_size_bytes=85155984`, and `total_runtime_seconds=289.715`. `python scripts/benchmark_edge_pdb_coverage.py --profile thesis --seed 2026 --cases-per-depth 16 --include-additive --artifact-suffix cpdb_additive` passed and wrote `results/processed/edge_pdb_coverage_seed_2026_thesis_cpdb_additive.json`, but `additive_improved_case_count=0` over 80 deterministic rows. This validates the additive/cost-partitioned code path and records that the first URF/DLB partition is too weak to improve the current sample.

Current native Korf upper-bound proof follow-up: the full-cube native Korf/IDA* solver exposes `--upper-bound-proof-strategy single-bound|iterative`. The optimized `single-bound` mode is exact-safe: with a verified upper solution of length `U`, it exhaustively searches the single bound `U-1`, keeps searching after any shorter solution is found, and reports exact only after the whole shorter-than-upper search space finishes. The Python wrapper, public `rubik-optimal solve --solver optimal-native`, and `scripts/run_3x3_optimal.py --native-upper-bound-proof-strategy` all forward this setting and preserve `upper_bound_proof_strategy`, `upper_bound_proof_search_bound`, `upper_bound_proof_exhaustive`, and `upper_bound_shorter_solution_found` in notes. The follow-up now also wires that native C++ proof into `PortfolioOptimalOracle` and `UniversalOptimalOracle`: after a verified upper solution and admissible H48 lower bound leave a small configured gap, the oracle can run the native Korf single-bound proof before full resident-H48 fallback. The public `solve --solver universal-optimal`, `oracle --universal`, `scripts/run_universal_oracle_cli.py`, and `scripts/run_portfolio_optimal_oracle.py` expose `--native-korf-upper-bound-proof-timeout` and `--native-korf-upper-bound-proof-max-gap`. Focused tests prove the portfolio returns `selected_backend=native-korf-upper-bound-proof` only for verified exact native proof results, and the regenerated H48 contracts record both `portfolio_oracle_can_use_native_korf_upper_bound_proof=true` and `public_oracle_cli_exposes_native_korf_upper_bound_proof=true`. H48H7 still passes; H48H10 still fails honestly because this proof integration does not create the missing 30.3 GiB H48H10 table or the 8 all-state-fast proof workloads.

Current direct nissy-core batch follow-up: `solve_nissy_core_direct_optimal_batch` now exposes an exact-safe batch API for source-less cubie states. The current nissy-core shell accepts one `-cube` command per process, so the wrapper does not claim true in-process direct-core residency; it does prepare the H48 table symlink once, preserves input order, annotates rows with `table_symlink_reused=true` and `process_per_row=true`, and verifies every solution locally before returning `exact`. `PortfolioOptimalOracle.solve_many` now sends all pending state-input rows through this batch helper before the representative-scramble Nissy batch. Focused tests prove the batch helper preserves order and that the portfolio no longer calls the per-row direct-core helper for solve-many state rows. The regenerated H48H7 contract records `portfolio_oracle_batches_nissy_core_direct_for_state_input=true` and `external_nissy_core_direct_batch_backend_reuses_h48_table_symlink=true`; H48H10 still fails honestly with `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false`.

Current resident nissy-core Python follow-up: the local nissy-core Python extension now builds for the repo's Conda Python 3.12 using the macOS dynamic-lookup linker path, and `.codex_external/nissy-core/python/nissy_module.c` exposes `solve_buffer` so contiguous Python buffers can be passed to `nissy_solve` without requiring a `bytearray`. `NissyCoreDirectPythonSession` keeps a child Python worker alive with one selected nissy table; `nissy_core_worker.py` opens the table with `mmap.ACCESS_READ`, uses `solve_buffer` when available, and falls back to `bytearray` only for older modules. `solve_nissy_core_direct_optimal_batch` now auto-allows large tables through the Python resident path when the module supports `solve_buffer`, while keeping the conservative `RUBIK_OPTIMAL_NISSY_CORE_PYTHON_MAX_TABLE_BYTES` gate for bytearray-only modules. Real smoke evidence now covers both h48h0 and h48h7: the h48h7 direct-state smoke solved `R U` exactly/verified through `nissy_core_python_resident_h48h7` with `table_data_mode=mmap`, `solve_buffer_available=True`, and worker runtime 2.144022s under one thread. The regenerated H48H7 contract records `external_nissy_core_python_resident_auto_allows_large_mmap_tables=true`; H48H10 still fails honestly because this does not create the missing stronger table or all-state proof workloads.

Current universal RubikOptimal prepass follow-up: `UniversalOptimalOracle.solve_many` now routes the batch RubikOptimal prepass through the same top-level resident `RubikOptimalOracleSession` used by the single-state prepass and late fallback, instead of starting a separate RubikOptimal batch-helper subprocess. Rows receive bounded per-row shares of the global prepass budget, and notes record `universal_prepass_uses_shared_rubikoptimal_session=true`, `prepass_row_timeout_seconds`, `prepass_global_timeout_seconds`, and `rubikoptimal_resident_start_count`. Focused regressions prove the resident prepass solves all rows before H48 fallback, and that RubikOptimal symmetry batch/race still runs after a resident prepass timeout. The regenerated H48H7 and H48H10 contracts record `universal_oracle_rubikoptimal_prepass_uses_shared_resident_session=true`; H48H7 still passes and H48H10 still fails honestly with `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false` because its trusted table and proof workloads are absent.

Current universal RubikOptimal symmetry-batch follow-up: the sequential RubikOptimal symmetry batch path now also uses the top-level resident `RubikOptimalOracleSession` instead of importing or calling the separate RubikOptimal batch-helper subprocess. This applies both to single-state `UniversalOptimalOracle.solve` and corpus `solve_many` when `rubikoptimal_symmetry_max_concurrency=0`; the intentionally parallel rotational race path remains separate. Row notes record `universal_symmetry_batch_uses_shared_rubikoptimal_session=true`, `symmetry_row_timeout_seconds`, `symmetry_global_timeout_seconds`, and `rubikoptimal_resident_start_count`. Focused tests prove resident symmetry batch reuse after a resident prepass timeout and when the prepass is disabled with identity rotation included. Regenerated H48H7/H48H10 contracts record `universal_oracle_rubikoptimal_symmetry_batch_uses_shared_resident_session=true`; H48H7 still passes and H48H10 still fails honestly with `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false`.

Current shared symmetry-ordering budget audit follow-up: the public universal-oracle timeout estimator and the known-distance-20 sweep already budget shared H48 lower-bound rotation ordering across resident H48, Nissy, direct nissy-core, parallel H48, and RubikOptimal symmetry phases. The contract now explicitly machine-checks that shared ordering budget path and the known-distance suffix/budget wiring through `universal_oracle_cli_budgets_shared_symmetry_ordering=true` and `known_distance_sweep_budgets_shared_symmetry_ordering=true`. Focused and broader affected tests pass, H48H7 regenerates with `passed=true`, and H48H10 still fails honestly with `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false` because the trusted H48H10 table and fast-target proof workloads remain absent.
Current native H48 generator optimization follow-up: the vendored H48 table generator now updates packed H48 pval and pval-min bytes with atomic compare-exchange min operations instead of taking a table-mutex lock for every mark/read in the DFS hot path. A real script-backed H48H0 regeneration through `scripts/generate_h48_tables.py --profile thesis --seed 2026 --solver h48h0 --threads 5 --mmap-output --progress-log --gendata-workbatch 16 --force` completed in 25.035260s, produced the same SHA-256 `46dcee74f59cf61852ac55acc7e1f5c6ec5acc63e2bf67320ee338466fb34f53`, and refreshed `results/processed/h48_metadata_seed_2026_thesis_h48h0.json`. The regenerated H48H7 and H48H10 contracts now record `h48_native_generation_uses_atomic_table_updates=true`; H48H7 still passes, while H48H10 still fails honestly with `fast_runtime_proven_for_every_possible_state=false` because the trusted H48H10 table and required proof workloads are absent.

Current native H48 lock-free scheduling follow-up: no AWS server was created or used, and no H48H8/H48H10 heavy generation was launched. The vendored H48 generator now assigns short-cube work batches and reports scanned/processed counters through C11 atomic fetch-add/load helpers instead of serializing those operations behind `shortcubes_mutex`. A real script-backed H48H0 regeneration through `scripts/generate_h48_tables.py --profile thesis --seed 2026 --solver h48h0 --threads 5 --mmap-output --progress-log --gendata-workbatch 256 --force` completed in 26.834345s, scanned all 10,000,019 slots, processed all 7,437,855 short cubes, and produced the same SHA-256 `46dcee74f59cf61852ac55acc7e1f5c6ec5acc63e2bf67320ee338466fb34f53`. The regenerated H48H7/H48H10 contracts now record `h48_native_generation_uses_atomic_work_scheduling=true`; H48H7 still passes, and H48H10 still fails honestly because the trusted H48H10 table and eight proof workload artifacts remain absent.

Current native H48 short-cube symmetry follow-up: no AWS, SSH, H48H8, or H48H10 heavy generation was launched. The vendored H48 short-cube generator now expands symmetry-equivalent H48 coordinates with the same edge-only coordinate path already used by the main H48 marker: compute the cocsep class/representative once, transform only edges to the representative, iterate self-symmetries, and insert `coord_h48_edges_rep(edge_sym, coclass, 11)`. This removed the stale `FOREACH_H48SIM` full-cube macro/TODO from `gendata_types_macros.h`. A real H48H0 regeneration through `scripts/generate_h48_tables.py --profile thesis --seed 2026 --solver h48h0 --threads 5 --mmap-output --progress-log --gendata-workbatch 256 --force` completed in 22.547087s, scanned all 10,000,019 slots, processed all 7,437,855 short cubes, and produced the same 31,683,944-byte table with SHA-256 `46dcee74f59cf61852ac55acc7e1f5c6ec5acc63e2bf67320ee338466fb34f53`. The regenerated H48H7/H48H10 contracts record `h48_native_short_generation_uses_edge_only_symmetry_expansion=true`; H48H7 still passes, and H48H10 still fails honestly because no trusted H48H10 table or eight proof workload artifacts exist.

Current Nissy batch timeout-preservation follow-up: `solve_nissy_light_optimal_batch` now runs the Nissy batch process with a streaming stdout/stderr reader instead of relying on `subprocess.run(..., timeout=...)` partial output. Completed rows are parsed as soon as their solution line is available, and if a later row times out the wrapper keeps those exact verified rows while marking only unfinished rows as `timeout`. The focused regressions prove completed-row preservation and shorter-scramble-first ordering under a subsecond fake-binary timeout. This strengthens long proof-batch evidence preservation, but it is not a new all-state runtime proof.

Current H48H8 probe instrumentation follow-up: no AWS server was created or used. The bounded local probes after the atomic-update generator still show that laptop H48H8 generation is not a practical all-state proof route. `results/processed/h48_generation_probe_seed_2026_thesis_h48h8_atomic60s.json` timed out after 60.251307s with no completed short-cube batch. `results/processed/h48_generation_probe_seed_2026_thesis_h48h8_atomic_workbatch1_180s.json` timed out after 180.031471s with 26 / 7,437,855 short cubes processed and an estimated 14,306.018 hours remaining at that measured rate. A native short-cube Bloom-prefilter experiment was then measured in `results/processed/h48_generation_probe_seed_2026_thesis_h48h8_bloom_workbatch1_180s.json`; it timed out after 182.493598s with only 22 / 7,437,855 short cubes processed and an estimated 17,138.345 hours remaining, so the Bloom-filter code was removed rather than retained as an optimization. The useful part kept from this pass is the bounded probe instrumentation: `scripts/probe_h48_generation_throughput.py --gendata-workbatch` now records `h48_gendata_workbatch`, and the regenerated H48 contracts machine-check `h48_generation_probe_records_native_workbatch=true`. H48H7 still passes; H48H10 still fails honestly because the trusted stronger table and proof workloads are absent.

Current H48 native generation scheduling follow-up: no AWS server was created or used.
```
